title: 🟢 Trees — ROI & Pattern
oneSentence: Navigate nodes deeply (DFS) or level-by-level (BFS) to solve hierarchical problems.
description: Master the foundational recursive DFS template. Most tree problems are just variations of this basic traversal.

roi.complexity: Time: O(N) | Space: O(H) where H is tree height
roi.signals: Hierarchical data, paths from root to leaf, level-by-level processing, Lowest Common Ancestor.

codeLines.text: // STEP 1 — BASE CASE: Empty node means depth is 0
// STEP 2 — DIVIDE: Recursively find the depth of the left subtree
// STEP 3 — DIVIDE: Recursively find the depth of the right subtree
// STEP 4 — CONQUER: The depth is 1 (for current node) + max of left/right subtrees

traceSteps.title: 1. Base Case
traceSteps.text: Every recursion needs a base case. For a tree, an empty node (null) returns a depth of 0.

traceSteps.title: 2. Divide (Left)
traceSteps.text: Recursively call the function on the left child to find the depth of the left subtree.

traceSteps.title: 3. Divide (Right)
traceSteps.text: Recursively call the function on the right child to find the depth of the right subtree.

traceSteps.title: 4. Conquer (Merge)
traceSteps.text: Combine the results: the depth is 1 (for the current node) plus the maximum depth between its left and right subtrees.

sandbox.question: Why is the base case check (`root == null`) necessary?
options: To prevent infinite recursion and NullPointerException.
To improve the time complexity to O(1).
It is not necessary; the compiler automatically checks for nulls.

sandbox.question: Why is the time complexity of this DFS traversal O(N)?
options: Because we visit leaf nodes only.
Because we visit each node in the tree exactly once.
Because the tree is perfectly balanced.
Because we do not visit all nodes.

sandbox.question: What dictates the space complexity O(H) where H is the tree height?
options: The maximum number of leaf nodes.
The maximum number of nodes at the bottom level.
The maximum depth of the recursive call stack.
The total number of nodes N.

followUps.q: Can you do this iteratively instead of recursively?
followUps.a: Yes, use an explicit `Stack` for DFS or a `Queue` for BFS. Iterative approaches prevent StackOverflowErrors on very deep trees.

followUps.q: What if it's an N-ary tree instead of binary?
followUps.a: Instead of `node.left` and `node.right`, iterate through `node.children` in a `for` loop during traversal.

followUps.q: How would you find LCA if parent pointers are available?
followUps.a: Treat it like finding the intersection of two Linked Lists. Traverse from both nodes up to the root to get their depths, equalize depths, then move up together until they meet.

followUps.q: DFS vs BFS — when to use which for tree problems?
followUps.a: Use BFS (Level Order) when searching for the shortest path or level-specific data. Use DFS for deep path logic, full traversals, or when memory is a concern (O(H) vs O(W)).

followUps.q: What if the tree is too large to fit on one machine?
followUps.a: You must distribute the graph/tree across machines and use distributed algorithms (like Pregel/MapReduce), or store it in a Graph Database and query it iteratively.
